intro: The clearest split between Stark and Fable is cost: Stark offers a free tier with paid upgrades (pricing not public), while Fable is free to use. Stark is design-tool accessibility plugin for Figma, Sketch, and Adobe XD (contrast, vision sim). Fable is human-led accessibility testing with disabled testers and user research. Pick Stark when the job is design teams catching a11y issues in figma before engineering and you accept medium lock-in; pick Fable when it is enterprises running inclusive research with disabled users alongside audits and you accept low lock-in. The data we have shows Stark at mostly transparent pricing and docs with average developer experience, and Fable at mostly transparent pricing and docs with average developer experience. The honest trade-off: neither is universal — Stark is a poor fit for teams wanting automated scans of production web apps, and Fable is a poor fit for dev teams just wanting automated axe-style scans in ci. Match the pricing model and lock-in level to how your team actually works, not the feature list.
quick_take: Stark is for design-tool accessibility plugin; Fable is for human-led accessibility testing; decide based on budget vs. support.
verdict_a: Choose Stark if a free tier with room to grow up front matters and the work maps to design-tool accessibility plugin for Figma, Sketch, and Adobe XD (contrast, vision sim).
verdict_b: Choose Fable if keeping the configuration portable matters and the work lines up with human-led accessibility testing with disabled testers and user research.

faq.a: Neither Stark nor Fable publishes entry pricing that lets us compare directly — Stark uses a freemium model and Fable uses a free model. Get quotes from both before assuming one is cheaper.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Stark to Fable?
faq.a: Migration is possible in either direction. Stark carries medium lock-in and Fable carries low lock-in, so plan for meaningful but not blocking rework. Run both in parallel before you fully cut over.

Both Stark and Fable rate the same on developer experience (3/5). The decision on DX then comes down to taste — which CLI, UI, or workflow matches your team's habits. A short side-by-side trial is the quickest way to tell.

faq.q: Is Fable a good alternative to Stark?
faq.a: Yes — Fable is a reasonable alternative to Stark for design teams catching a11y issues in figma before engineering.. The practical differences are freemium-vs-free billing and medium-vs-low lock-in. If those fit your constraints better, treat Fable as a credible swap.
